topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The cardboard box sat accusingly on the worn welcome mat. I stared at the address label, the handwriting familiar yet alien. It was from the apartment above, the one I’d shared a few quiet evenings with. A dull ache settled low in my chest, a heavy, leaden pressure that made each breath shallow. I picked up the box, the tape sticking stubbornly to my fingers. Inside, nestled in crumpled packing paper, was a collection of mismatched mugs and a worn paperback. A sudden, sharp sting behind my eyes made me blink rapidly, trying to clear the blurry edges of the room. I dropped the box, the muffled thud echoing too loudly in the silence. I walked to the window, the city lights blurring through a sheen of unshed tears, and just stood there, feeling utterly hollowed out.

[story 2]

When the delivery man handed me the package, his smile felt too bright, too casual. It was addressed to ‘J. Smith, Apt 3B.’ My stomach clenched, a tight, twisting knot of distress. I took the box with a trembling hand, the cheap paper feeling flimsy and inadequate against the weight of what it represented. Opening it was an act of pure, agonizing vulnerability. The contents were mundane—old takeout menus and a single, faded photograph. I didn't look at the items; my focus was entirely on the texture of the paper under my fingertips, trying to anchor myself against the sudden, dizzying sense of loss. I retreated to the kitchen, leaning against the counter until my legs felt weak, the silence of the apartment amplifying the frantic drumming inside my own ribs.

[story 3]

I found the package tucked behind the recycling bin, a stark, unwanted intrusion. My hands shook as I peeled back the packing tape. Seeing the return address—the name of the person who had just vacated—sent a cold shock through me that stole my breath. A strange tightness seized my throat, making it difficult to form a coherent sound. I sank onto the floor, the rough carpet scratching against my knees. Every muscle in my body seemed to have locked up; I couldn't move, couldn't look at the contents. I just sat, staring at the faded ink on the label, a profound emptiness expanding outward from my core, leaving me stranded in a quiet, suffocating space.
